did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used artificial intelligence, specifically deep learning models (MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet), to perform skin disease recognition (classification) on images of acne, actinic keratosis, benign tumors, eczema, and vitiligo.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing the diagnostic capabilities of three deep learning models (MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet) on a specific dataset of 4486 skin disease images. It includes detailed methodology, experimental configuration, and quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, AUC, F1 score) for the proposed study, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Disease Recognition Based on Computer Vision
journal: Proceedings of CONF-MLA 2025 Symposium: Intelligent Systems and Automation: AI Models, IoT, and Robotic Algorithms
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic capabilities of three deep learning models—MobileNetV2 (96×96, 0.35), MobileNetV2 (160×160, 0.75), and EfficientNet—for classifying five skin diseases: acne, actinic keratosis, benign tumors, eczema, and vitiligo. The dataset comprised 4,486 clinical images sourced from the DermNet database. Models were assessed using accuracy, AUC, and F1 scores, with EfficientNet achieving the highest test accuracy of 82% and an AUC of 0.98. In contrast, MobileNetV2 (96×96, 0.35) performed poorly with only 65% accuracy, while MobileNetV2 (160×160, 0.75) reached 77.7% accuracy. The results demonstrate that EfficientNet is the most effective model for this task, highlighting the practicality of AI in skin disease identification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the five experimental subjects; dataset contains 651 images (588 training, 63 test).

all_task_types.target: Five discrete skin disease categories: acne, actinic keratosis, benign tumors, eczema, and vitiligo.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin diseases using deep learning models (MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet) on clinical images. The task is evaluated using accuracy, AUC, F1 score, and confusion matrices, which are standard metrics for classification tasks. The models predict discrete diagnostic labels rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: DermNet Database
datasets_used.description: A public website containing pictures of various skin diseases. The images used in this study are clinical real-life shots in JPEG format with RGB color space, featuring variations in lighting, angle, and disease sites to simulate actual clinical scenarios.

use.training: Used for training the MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet models. The dataset was split into training and test sets per class. Additionally, a validation set accounting for 20% of the data was used during the training process for all models.
use.validation: A validation set comprising 20% of the data was used for model evaluation during training for all three models (MobileNetV2 96x96, MobileNetV2 160x160, and EfficientNet).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of model performance (accuracy, AUC, F1 score) after training.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate classification performance, including per-class accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and uncertainty rates for each skin disease category.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the discrimination ability of the models.

statistical_methods_used.method: Weighted Average Precision, Recall, and F1 Score
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide a comprehensive summary of model performance across all classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: Train Loss and Train Accuracy Curve Analysis
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze the training dynamics and convergence of the models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper compares model performance using accuracy, AUC, F1 score, and confusion matrices without statistical significance tests.

DermNet Database.notes: EfficientNet achieved the highest test accuracy (82%) and AUC (0.98). MobileNetV2 (96×96, 0.35) performed the worst with 65% test accuracy. MobileNetV2 (160×160, 0.75) showed intermediate performance with 77.7% test accuracy. All models used a validation set accounting for 20% of the data during training.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNetV2 (96×96, 0.35), MobileNetV2 (160×160, 0.75), and EfficientNet (specifically EfficientNet-B0 scaled to 80-4M params)
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNet used pretrained weights; MobileNetV2 pretraining details not explicitly reported.

MobileNetV2_96.features: Lightweight, reduced parameters and computation, suitable for low storage/power devices

MobileNetV2_160.features: Balance between efficiency and accuracy, improved spatial information capture and feature expression

EfficientNet.backbone: EfficientNet-B0 with composite scaling and SE attention module
EfficientNet.scaling: Composite scaling of depth, width, and resolution with fixed zoom factor φ
EfficientNet.features: SE attention module added to each MBConv module, Swish activation function, NAS-derived layer/module combination

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation: Rotate, flip, and crop operations applied to improve training robustness.
Input resizing: Images resized to 224×224 pixels for model input (original dataset images varied in size, e.g., 294×222 for most classes, 612×378 for Vitiligo).
Validation split: 20% of the data used as a validation set during training.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to images to improve training robustness; specific probabilities or angles not reported.

conclusions: EfficientNet achieved the highest diagnostic performance among the tested models, with a test accuracy of 82% and an AUC of 0.98, making it the most suitable model for skin disease recognition in this study.

MobileNetV2 (96×96, 0.35) performed poorly with only 65% test accuracy and was deemed unsuitable for skin disease detection due to limited feature extraction capabilities and high uncertainty rates for classes like acne (30.2%) and actinic keratosis (28.0%).

MobileNetV2 (160×160, 0.75) showed intermediate performance with 77.7% test accuracy and an AUC of 0.97, demonstrating that increasing input resolution and width factor improves performance over the smaller MobileNetV2 variant.

Vitiligo was the easiest class to classify across all models, achieving the highest per-class F1 scores (0.90 for MobileNetV2 96x96, 0.91 for MobileNetV2 160x160, and 0.94 for EfficientNet) and the lowest uncertainty rates.

Actinic keratosis presented classification challenges, particularly for EfficientNet where the uncertainty rate increased to 13.4%, potentially due to the model amplifying subtle image differences with higher resolution inputs.

The study demonstrates the practicality of artificial intelligence in skin disease identification, suggesting it can address the time-consuming and subjective nature of traditional naked-eye diagnosis by dermatologists.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, relatively small dataset (4,486 images) sourced exclusively from the public DermNet database, lacking external validation on independent clinical cohorts.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, with 'Benign Tumors' comprising 26.6% of the data while 'Vitiligo' comprises only 17.6%, which may bias model performance.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance differences between models, limiting the robustness of the comparative conclusions.

The MobileNetV2 (96×96, 0.35) model demonstrated poor performance (65% accuracy) and high uncertainty rates for Acne (30.2%) and Actinic Keratosis (28.0%), suggesting it is unsuitable for fine-grained dermatological classification.

The EfficientNet model showed increased uncertainty for Actinic Keratosis (13.4%) compared to MobileNetV2 variants, potentially due to the amplification of subtle image differences by higher-resolution inputs.

Vitiligo was consistently the easiest class to classify across all models (highest F1 scores and lowest uncertainty), indicating potential dataset bias where one class is disproportionately easier to learn than others.

The study claims to use 'clinical real-life shooting' images with variations in lighting and angle, but does not provide detailed demographic information (e.g., skin type distribution) of the subjects, raising concerns about generalizability across diverse populations.

The paper is a conference proceeding with limited methodological detail regarding hyperparameter optimization beyond fixed settings, and does not discuss potential data leakage or rigorous cross-validation strategies.
